what_it_does: Renders a fixed 1600x900 HTML composition explaining a movement-line visual language through four illustrated panels: Transport, Gather In, Gather Out, and Descend.
observations: Uses a centered .view canvas with a radial warm/cool background and faint blueprint grid decoration.
Loads Plus Jakarta Sans from Google Fonts and design tokens from /surface/styles/vars.css.
Title area reads "Dashed Means Moving" and "Three Verbs, One Line Language".
Four grid cells each contain a heading, short explanatory subtitle, and inline SVG diagram.
Dashed bronze lines are used consistently to depict movement paths.
Gold shapes mark destinations, payloads, or arrowheads in the diagrams.
Includes a circular "V" mark near the lower right and a textured footer wash.
Contains no JavaScript and no declared code symbols.
standouts: The file is primarily an instructional visual grammar slide rather than an interactive page.
The same dashed-line convention is demonstrated across sibling transport, convergence, fan-out, and descent.
Gather is represented as two directional cases while the headline frames the set as three verbs.
conventions: Uses a global CSS reset and fixed-size 16:9 composition.
Relies heavily on shared CSS custom properties with dna-prefixed spacing, radius, and type tokens.
Places page-level elements with absolute positioning and lays the main panels out with CSS grid.
Keeps illustrations inline as SVG with CSS-variable colors.
Uses concise panel labels and subtitles instead of long explanatory copy.
concerns: The fixed 1600x900 canvas is not responsive within this file.
The page depends on external font loading and /surface/styles/vars.css for its intended appearance.
Inline SVGs have no accessible titles or descriptions.
Several diagram styling patterns are repeated directly rather than factored within the file.
notes: A static instructional slide showing how dashed lines encode movement verbs in a visual system.
questions: Does the "V" mark identify a version, series marker, or section marker?
Is "Gather, In" and "Gather, Out" intended to count as one verb split into two directional examples?
Is this file meant to be viewed only in a fixed slide renderer?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the content: the page teaches a dashed-line movement language through labeled verb diagrams, with gather shown in two directions.
intention_signals: It appears intended to define or teach a compact visual vocabulary for how entities move between, into, out of, or down into destinations.
novelty: The file treats movement as a small set of diagram verbs and uses presentation-slide styling to make the notation feel like a designed grammar.
contribution: Contributes a clear dashed-line motion convention, four concrete movement diagrams, and a warm technical slide treatment that a fused system could retain as an explanatory visual substrate.
summary_for_embedding: A fixed-size HTML slide with inline SVGs that explains dashed-line movement verbs through Transport, Gather In, Gather Out, and Descend panels.
intention_for_embedding: This was for briefing or documenting a visual line language where dashed paths encode movement and gold marks indicate arrival or direction.
